names.japanese_name: ソルビン酸ナトリウム

natural_occurrence.description: The sodium salt of sorbic acid. Sorbic acid was first isolated in 1859 from rowan berries, though today all commercial production is synthetic. Unlike potassium and calcium sorbates, sodium sorbate is banned in the EU due to genotoxicity concerns.

manufacturing.description: Produced by neutralizing synthetically manufactured sorbic acid with sodium hydroxide or sodium carbonate. Sorbic acid is synthesized through the condensation of crotonaldehyde with ketene.

other_uses.cosmetics: Limited use due to safety concerns
other_uses.pharmaceutical: Not commonly used due to EU ban

eu.conditions: BANNED in EU since 1998 due to genotoxic concerns. Previously approved in Germany until 1998.

usa.notes: Permitted for use in cheese, fruit butter, fruit jelly, fruit preserves and jams, margarine

japan.japanese_regulation_name: ソルビン酸ナトリウム
japan.notes: Part of sorbic acid group with usage standards

canada.notes: Permitted for use in cheese and other specified foods

safety_evaluations.jecfa_adi: 0–25 mg/kg bw/day (group ADI for sorbic acid and sorbates, expressed as sorbic acid)

regulatory_history.event: Sorbic acid first isolated from rowan berries by A.W. von Hofmann
Antimicrobial properties of sorbic acid discovered
JECFA establishes group ADI of 25 mg/kg bw/day for sorbic acid and sorbates
Banned in EU due to genotoxicity concerns; previously approved in Germany
Study confirms genotoxicity to human peripheral blood lymphocytes in vitro at high concentrations

commonly_found_in: Cheese (USA, Canada only - banned in EU)

equivalents.product: servings of preserved fruit (100g at typical levels)

adi_context.source_note: Based on JECFA group ADI for sorbic acid and sorbates. NOTE: Banned in EU due to genotoxicity concerns despite JECFA approval. Other sorbates (E202, E203) remain approved globally.
